learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, HashMap, Sliding Window, Two Pointers, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine walking through an orchard carrying two baskets. Each basket can hold only one fruit type. As you walk from left to right, you keep collecting fruits until you encounter a third fruit type that cannot fit into either basket.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine walking through an orchard carrying two baskets. Each basket can hold only one fruit type. As you walk from left to right, you keep collecting fruits until you encounter a third fruit type that cannot fit into either basket.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Variable Size Sliding Window pattern whenever a problem asks for the longest or shortest subarray satisfying a constraint on distinct elements.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem asks for the longest contiguous subarray with at most K distinct elements, think of Variable Size Sliding Window.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are visiting a farm that has a row of fruit trees represented by an integer array fruits, where fruits[i] is the type of fruit produced by the ith tree. You have exactly two baskets, and each basket can hold only one type of fruit. Starting from any tree, collect exactly one fruit from every tree while moving to the right until you encounter a third fruit type. Return the maximum number of fruits you can collect.

examples.explanation: Both baskets hold fruit types 1 and 2, so all fruits can be collected.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Generate every possible subarray and count distinct fruit types until more than two types appear.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: The problem asks for the longest contiguous subarray containing at most two distinct numbers. Maintain a sliding window while tracking the frequency of fruit types inside the window.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: The problem is equivalent to finding the longest subarray containing at most two distinct elements.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem asks for the longest contiguous subarray with at most K distinct elements, think of Variable Size Sliding Window.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each tree enters and leaves the sliding window at most once. Therefore, every element is processed a constant number of times, resulting in linear time complexity.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem asks for the longest contiguous subarray with at most K distinct elements, think of Variable Size Sliding Window.
commonWrongPaths: Not shrinking the window when three fruit types appear.
Forgetting to remove fruit types with zero frequency.
Using a Set instead of tracking frequencies.
Confusing distinct fruits with total fruits.

hints: Shrink the window when more than two fruit types appear.
commonMisconceptions: Not shrinking the window when three fruit types appear.
Forgetting to remove fruit types with zero frequency.
Using a Set instead of tracking frequencies.
Confusing distinct fruits with total fruits.

pattern.name: Variable Size Sliding Window
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem asks for the longest contiguous subarray with at most K distinct elements, think of Variable Size Sliding Window.

approaches.title: Check Every Subarray
approaches.idea: Generate every possible subarray and count distinct fruit types until more than two types appear.
algorithm: Update maximum length if distinct types are at most two.

code.java: Brute force omitted because every subarray must be checked.

approaches.title: Sliding Window with HashMap
approaches.idea: Maintain a sliding window containing at most two fruit types using a HashMap to store frequencies.
algorithm: Shrink while more than two fruit types exist.

approaches.title: Variable Size Sliding Window
approaches.idea: Expand the right pointer while maintaining the frequencies of fruit types inside a HashMap. Whenever the window contains more than two distinct fruit types, shrink the window from the left until only two remain. Track the maximum window size throughout the traversal.
approaches.intuition: Each tree enters and leaves the sliding window at most once. Therefore, every element is processed a constant number of times, resulting in linear time complexity.
algorithm: Add current fruit to the map.
While the map contains more than two fruit types:
Remove it if frequency becomes zero.
approaches.dryRun:
fruits=[1,2,3,2,2]
Window=[1,2]
Add 3 → 3 types
Shrink → [2,3]
Extend → [2,3,2,2]
Length=4

visualization.description: Maintain a window containing at most two fruit types.
steps: Shrink if more than two types appear.

interviewFollowUps: What if three baskets are available?
Can this be generalized to K distinct elements?
How is this related to Longest Substring with K Distinct Characters?
Can an array be solved without HashMap?
What if fruits arrive as a stream?
interviewTips: Recognize that this is 'Longest Subarray with At Most Two Distinct Elements'.
Explain why frequencies are required instead of a Set.
Shrink only when the window becomes invalid.
Update the answer after restoring validity.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: The problem is equivalent to finding the longest subarray containing at most two distinct elements.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Expand Window → Shrink if Distinct > 2 → Track Maximum.

importantPoints: Window must contain at most two fruit types.
Every element enters and leaves once.
commonMistakes: Not shrinking the window when three fruit types appear.
Forgetting to remove fruit types with zero frequency.
Using a Set instead of tracking frequencies.
Confusing distinct fruits with total fruits.

relatedProblems: Longest Substring with At Most Two Distinct Characters
Longest Substring with K Distinct Characters
